key_insight: Models can turn their own test-time reasoning traces into reusable latent memories, enabling continual self-improvement without external labels or future data.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a practical, low-overhead path to adaptive reasoning systems that improve online with only a tiny number of trainable parameters.
method: Use self-generated test-time signals, such as majority voting, as rewards for lightweight per-instance learning.
Distill transient reasoning compute into compact modular soft-prompt memories rather than reusing raw traces directly.
Store and retrieve these latent memories for future inputs to support continual learning while reducing catastrophic forgetting.
evidence_signal: Across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, the method outperforms zero-shot and raw in-context learning baselines and transfers effectively across datasets.
watch_item: The approach depends on reliable self-generated signals and careful memory management, so performance may degrade if majority votes are noisy or retrieval is poorly matched.
paper_title: Continual Self-Improvement with Lightweight Experiential Latent Memories
